title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
section: Shared framing: climate projections, uncertainty, and interpretation
text:
Shared framing: climate projections, uncertainty, and interpretation


Shared framing


Climate projections, uncertainty, and interpretation

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
Why start here?

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Why start here?

Participants bring different backgrounds: climate modeling, marine ecology, data curation, species modeling, and conservation planning. This opening section aligns expectations before we interpret data products or SDM outputs.

We need a shared language before discussing model outputs.
We need to separate evidence, assumptions, and interpretation.
We need feedback that improves the workflow, not just the slides.


isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
The objective is shared interpretation

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


The objective is shared interpretation

This section is not technical training. It establishes a common framing for discussions throughout the workshop.

What can climate projections tell us?
What should not be over-interpreted?
Where do assumptions enter the workflow?
How should outputs support conservation planning?


isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
Projections are conditional futures

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Climate projections vs predictions

Climate projections are not deterministic predictions. They describe plausible futures conditional on emissions pathways, model structure, realizations, downscaling choices, and interpretation assumptions.


Climate scenario


Model family


Ensemble member


Downscaled product


Conservation interpretation



isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
section: Uncertainty is part of responsible use
text:
Uncertainty is part of responsible use

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Interpretation of uncertainty

Uncertainty is not a failure of the workflow. It is information about where interpretation should be careful, where additional data would help, and where decisions should remain robust to multiple plausible futures.


Climate and ocean products

model and scenario uncertainty
ensemble member variability
downscaling assumptions



Ecological interpretation

biological sampling bias
SDM algorithm uncertainty
conservation-use uncertainty




isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
SDMs depend on assumptions

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Limitations and assumptions associated with SDMs

SDMs connect species records to environmental predictors, but the result depends on the data, predictor choices, modeling algorithm, spatial scale, and interpretation of habitat suitability.

Records are not always evenly sampled across space or time.
Environmental predictors are simplifications of ecological processes.
Model performance does not automatically imply ecological truth.
Outputs are strongest when interpreted with expert knowledge.


isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
Model outputs are planning inputs

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Appropriate use in conservation planning

Model outputs should support conservation discussion, prioritization, and scenario thinking. They should not be treated as final maps of where conservation action must happen.

Use outputs to compare patterns and identify questions.
Communicate confidence and uncertainty alongside maps.
Avoid over-interpreting fine-scale boundaries.
Link model interpretation to feasible planning decisions.


isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
Working rule for the workshop

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Working rule for the workshop

Model outputs should guide discussion, prioritization, and improvement. They should not be treated as final conservation truth without considering data coverage, model assumptions, and uncertainty.


Where are the patterns useful?
Where are they fragile?
Which assumptions need to be stated more clearly?
What would make the next version more decision-relevant?



isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

From data to climate-informed SDMs

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


From data to climate-informed SDMs

This presentation connects physical ocean projections, biological observations, and first-generation species distribution models into a transparent workflow for expert review.


Physical ocean data


Biological records


Predictor extraction


First-generation SDMs


Planning interpretation


The intended outcome is focused feedback on data gaps, species/model interpretation, and realistic improvements over the next year.

text:
Physical modeling and downscaling framework

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Physical modeling and downscaling framework

This section summarizes the ocean products and workflow logic used to connect present-day baselines and future climate conditions to SDM predictors.

Contemporary ocean conditions and future climate projections
Horizontal and vertical products relevant to SB5 species
Delta/change-field downscaling to trusted baseline products
Curated layers designed for visualization and SDM extraction
Viewer: Ocean downscaling Shiny app

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
section: What is a physical climate model?
text:
What is a physical climate model?

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


What is a physical climate model?



Physical models represent the ocean, atmosphere, land, and ice on numerical grids. They simulate energy and mass exchange through time, producing variables that can be summarized for ecological modeling.

horizontal grid
vertical layers
physical and biogeochemical processes
present-day and future time windows

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
Future work for physical products

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Future work for physical products

The current viewer and pipeline provide a transparent foundation. The next step is to expand the ensemble and make uncertainty easier to interpret.

More model families
More climate scenarios
More realizations / ensemble members
More depth-layer and pelagic summaries
Clearer uncertainty summaries for SDM interpretation

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
section: Status of biological data for SDMs
text:
Status of biological data for SDMs

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Status of biological data for SDMs

The biological data workflow preserves, harmonizes, quality-controls, and exports telemetry and observation records before SDM modeling.

Raw data are kept auditable and unchanged
Telemetry and observations are standardized separately
SDM-ready files preserve provenance and QC decisions
Final model inputs keep marine SDM-compatible coordinates
Viewer: Megafauna data viewer Shiny app


Local reference: shared/references/repos/pre-sdm-bio-data-prep-readme.md

isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
Data readiness is not uniform

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Data readiness is not uniform

The goal is to make data strengths and limitations visible before modeling decisions are made.

Stronger data support more confident model interpretation
Sparse data require simpler or more cautious interpretation
Biased sampling can shape apparent habitat relationships
Expert feedback is needed to prioritize realistic improvements



42,475 filtered records in viewer


8 SB5 species in current app view


71 data sources


2 data types: telemetry and observations

Initial outputs, performance, limitations, and model-improvement priorities

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
First generation of SDMs

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


First generation of SDMs

These outputs are preliminary to intermediate products intended to guide the next phase of model improvement.

Present initial model outputs
Review performance and ecological plausibility
Identify limitations and assumptions
Separate data gaps from model gaps
Prioritize realistic improvements over the next year


isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
Interpreting first-generation outputs

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Interpreting first-generation outputs

When reviewing early SDM outputs, focus on whether patterns are ecologically plausible, data-supported, and useful for planning conversations.

What patterns seem robust?
What patterns need caution?
Where does expert knowledge disagree with the model?
Which improvements would most strengthen final outputs?


isaaksonbm · ibrito@conservation.org

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
Feedback should be specific

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Feedback should be specific

The most useful feedback identifies what can realistically improve the final outputs within the project timeline.


Data feedback

missing sources
spatial or temporal gaps
questionable records
species-specific sampling issues



Model feedback

implausible spatial patterns
missing predictors
scale or depth concerns
uncertainty communication

text:
Future work

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Future work

The next phase should expand uncertainty representation and strengthen the connection between model outputs and conservation decisions.

More climate models
More climate scenarios
More realizations and ensemble summaries
Better uncertainty propagation into SDMs
Improved biological coverage and sampling-bias treatment
Clearer interpretation guidance for planning use

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
Discussion questions

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Discussion questions



Which data gaps matter most for the project timeline?
Which species or regions should be prioritized?
Which uncertainties must be communicated most clearly?
What outputs would be most useful for conservation planning?

title: SB5 Climate SDM Presentations
text:
Take-home message

IKI - Salvando a los Cinco Azules (SB5)


Take-home message

The workshop is not only about showing model outputs. It is about making the workflow transparent enough that experts can improve the data, interpret the models responsibly, and identify the next most important steps.


Shared framing


Physical products


Biological data readiness


First-generation SDMs


Focused feedback
